chunk_summary: This section outlines phenotype-based dietary interventions for adolescents, focusing on thinness, obesity, and RED-S risk. It provides actionable add/swap rules and executable day patterns tailored to growth and weight phenotypes, using WHO BMI-for-age classifications as a foundation.
learning_objectives: Understand how to classify adolescent nutritional status using WHO BMI-for-age standards.
Learn phenotype-specific dietary add and swap rules for thinness, obesity, and RED-S risk.
Develop executable day patterns for adolescent nutrition counseling in clinical settings.
Recognize the importance of monitoring and follow-up in adolescent dietary interventions.
key_facts: Thinness is defined as BMI-for-age <-2 SD (severe thinness <-3 SD) per WHO standards.
For thinness, add two energy-dense snacks daily and increase meal energy density with 1–2 teaspoons of oil/ghee if no malabsorption is present.
Obesity interventions include removing sugar-sweetened beverages, limiting free sugars to <10% of energy (ideally <5%), and maintaining protein and vegetable portions.
RED-S risk management includes mandatory pre-training fuel and recovery snacks within 1–2 hours, avoiding weight cutting and stimulants in minors.
Set measurable targets for obesity management: 60 minutes/day of activity, 8–10 hours of sleep, and zero sugary beverages.
reference_quote: WHO BMI-for-age: thinness is <-2 SD (severe <-3 SD); obesity should be staged and comorbidity risk assessed.
